speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two, interview with Ellen Leviatin Kaidanow. September the 20th, 1995. I'd like to spend a little more time on your memories of the bunker, even though you say you were only in there once as-- in hiding. What was in-- what were kept in there? Did you have linens in there or foods?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that we had dried foods-- foods that could last. Like, you know, I remember them drying bread. They used to call them [YIDDISH]. You know? Dried out bread which lasts longer than fresh bread. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what else do I remember? Potatoes. I remember that. That's the only thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there equipment in which to cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they had like a burner. You know, one of those-- the burners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. There was electricity in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I really don't know whether-- in the house there was electricity. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about water? Liquids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Water-- they saved, you know, a lot of water. And as I said to you, they did have some kind of pills where they could like under dire circumstances probably convert urine to water. I mean, to drink. Make it drinkable somehow. I don't know. I remember that, I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about medication?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember any medication or what else they put.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pills? Aspirin? Band-Aids?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure Aspirin. Band-Aids they didn't have in those days. But Aspirin definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What facilities were there for sanitary facilities were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't remember. They must have had a hole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a signal system whereby you would know when the aktsia was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only that I remember some-- whoever it was, like you just run in and say, they're coming. Somebody must have been on the outside that knew that they were marching, you know, towards the ghetto and let us know. Who it was, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when it was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it was over and the Germans left-- because as I said to you, I don't remember the Germans being in the ghetto at any time steady. Only when they came in to kill, and loot, or whatever, or if they needed to take things. So somebody let us know. And I remember they used to say, [YIDDISH]. You know, like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. But going out-- when they left, did you know or did you play it--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how they knew that. Whether it was just, you know, a feeling, and then they came out or took a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember leaving the bunker that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember leaving the bunker. And I remember that there were people that were killed. I remember them saying, you know, that one was killed. This one was killed. I don't know the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left was your-- and you came back into--

speaker: interviewee
text: The house.

speaker: interviewer
text: The part of the house, were there any changes in the house? Was anything destroyed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in our case. I don't remember anything destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother never went in with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever come face to face contact with Germans yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw them. I definitely saw Germans. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how I saw them, but I-- they-- it's like I could see it in my face. They were not dressed as generals. Just with their caps, and you know, shoes, and uniforms. Regular soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any aktsias where you were around rather than in a bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first one we were not in a bunker. We were in the house. And you just, you know, sort of heard shots and things like that. The second one we were in the bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to leave your house for that aktsia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. As I said to you, they mainly sort of-- whoever they caught in the street it seems that-- you know, outside. That I don't ever remember them coming into the house. It was mostly like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you mentioned--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or they would take out people, let's say, to work, and they never returned. Those were other things that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you mentioned that children were not informed about what was going on necessarily, but you were living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were escape or plans of any sort or rumors of any sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to tell you my own story, this very same Lena was willing to take a child-- you know, to save a child. And my father said, OK, you know, I'll give you one child. And it was me. And I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they pick you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a good question. I don't know. You know, it's very strange because my daughter, Pamela, came here last night, and I showed her some of my pictures. And all of a sudden she said to me, Ma, how come it was you? And I couldn't believe it. Because she like went into my own head, because many, many, many a times I wonder why it was me. You could interpret it any way. You could say-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a special relationship with Lena?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I mean, I know I loved her. But I'm sure the others did, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at this particular time, which was after the second aktsia and they sort of saw already what's going on. They decided to send me out to Lena. And I remember, like through a dream, going to that gate where it was attended by Jewish-- you could call them policeman or whatever they were. And my father kissing me goodbye. And I was like sort of crazy about my father. That's the one thing I remember. And he was crying. So I went out with her. And in the middle of the night, she had me at someplace in the neighbors or something. I ran away from her, and I came back to the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- I remember the next day, like in the morning, Lena came to the gate and she said, tell Joseph that I lost-- I don't know what happened to her. I lost the child. And he said, don't worry. She's here. So I was back in the ghetto. And the other thing I remember, which I'll never forget, the guy at the gate when I came back empty handed, he said, [YIDDISH SPEECH]. I didn't know what it was all about. But then I realized that whoever was on the outside had to come back with something-- a piece of bread, a bar of soap, anything. And here I come back from the world-- living world and I bring nothing. And I never forgot that words. It was just said like this, not even directly to me. I just sort of overheard it. And so that was the end of that. That was the first time that I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was where Lena was staying with you from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It couldn't have been too far, because I was able to run back myself. I don't know how I did it, but that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish police did not question your leaving or your coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they didn't. No, they did not. I guess we were all known to each other. You know, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that we were all in the ghetto. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how we lived, what we did, I truly don't remember. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- it came a time where they said it was the last aktsia, which means that they were liquidating the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a fact or a rumor?

speaker: interviewee
text: A fact. And what I remember is this-- that everybody ran to hide. My whole family, and that brother of his, and his family ran into the bunker. And all I remember is that they wanted my grandmother, Sarah, to go into the bunker. And I just stood there like this. And I said, if she's not going, I'm not going. And with that, somebody ran in and they said, Joseph, Lena is at the gate. Get one child out. Save one child. So they grabbed me and actually carried me to the gate. And it was a big to do-- because you know, as I said, they knew in advance that they were marching towards the ghetto. And they gave me over to Lena. And I remember she took me like this by the hand, and we just walked. Just walked straight like this. And I don't know how far we walked. And suddenly, we were stopped by the Ukrainian police. They took us into a room. I remember it distinctly that part of it. Because I guess by then I was a little older, so I remember more. And I was sitting with her, and they were questioning her. And the idea was-- I guess preconceived from the first time-- that she had me as an illegitimate child while working for the [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a Ukrainian policeman was going to question us. And suddenly, he was called away. There was a woman there scrubbing the floor. And she said, this is your chance. Get out of here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she took me by the hand, and we just walked. Just like that. And that was it. And we just walked, and walked, and-- towards her village where her parents lived. That's where we were going to go. And I do remember that eventually we sort of hitchhiked on a truck, you know. And we got to her village.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not remember the name of the village. In the village, was her mother. She did not have a father. An old woman-- a wonderful woman-- and a brother. And I don't even remember what kind of children he had. But I know-- I don't even know if he was married or not. But mainly, I remember the brother and the mother. And we came to them, Lena and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you remember how long it took you to get to the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: A long time. It was a very long time. You know, a long time walking and then by truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: By a long time, was it several days? Several hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I would say-- I would say probably at least a full day, and we arrived at night. So maybe a day and a night. Something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you any idea where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I just was holding her hand and walking with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that you were going to be with her for any length of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I suppose that probably half of the time it was like in shock. But I wasn't scared, because I was with her, and I did love her. And this time, I guess I maybe subconsciously I realized that-- you know, I don't know. I don't remember thinking that, gee, I'm going to live, or what's going to happen to my parents? I don't remember thinking that. I just went with her. Like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what I was wearing. A dress, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it winter? Summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was, I think, fall. It was not cold. I don't remember being cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any clothing with you at all? Anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just what I was wearing. Just like this. Whatever I was wearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did Lena tell you what she was doing-- or did she?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was just sort of understood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language where you speaking?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time it was Yiddish. She spoke Jewish fluent.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were being questioned or to be questioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just didn't answer. Like I-- you know. I wasn't questioned, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they were questioning her.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were questioning. They were going to question her in detail. What's-- you know, we prepared-- I mean, not we. But the story was prepared in such a way that I'm her daughter, and that she's going home to her village.

speaker: interviewer
text: She prepared the story herself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if they talked. I'm sure she didn't think of it herself. It must've been a combination of Father, and Mother, and whoever. You know, because as I said, I had-- I had gone before, just I came back. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was prearranged what she's going to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you had gone before, did you know that your name was going to be changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point did she tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Not until we got to her parents-- not parents, her mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, aren't you-- say you were on a truck part of the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who picked you up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, were they Germans, Ukrainians, Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Ukrainians. No, no, no. Ukrainians. For sure Ukrainians. It was the-- you know, one of the farmers or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak any Ukrainian at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, not really. Maybe just a little bit here and there. But not really, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impression of when you got to the village, the name of which you don't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: First impression? I-- really, you know, just that I came there. And the first thing they did is hide me, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know what a pripechek is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You describe it though, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first day, that's where I slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what it--

speaker: interviewee
text: What it is is that they have like a built in oven. And above the oven are like a cement ledge, but it's behind the oven. So it keeps warm, you know, when it's on, of course. And that's where I was sleeping there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In back of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: In back, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of the pripechek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given any instructions not to talk? Not to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think that somehow you just-- you know it. You just know it, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Lena stay with you in that pripechek? In the back of the pripechek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. She was with me constantly. I was like so attached to her that I didn't let her go anywhere. I didn't let-- you know, I was just with her all the time. I mean, probably not constant, but most of the time I was with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you hidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this place we stayed a very short time, because her brother was very much afraid that if they discovered that I am there-- and they knew that I was Jewish. They knew that I wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean her family knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her family knew. Her family absolutely knew. So they were terribly afraid that once they've discovered, of course, the ending would be the same for all of us-- and that's death. So what should we do? After a couple-- I don't know if it was a couple of weeks or a couple-- it had to be a couple of days. I don't know. Maybe two weeks. Something of that length of time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Lena said, what am I going to do? I'll go back. She was intending to bring me back to the ghetto not knowing-- we, at that time, didn't know that the ghetto was decimated and liquidated. And when she got there, there was no one there. She took you with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she left me. She did not take me with her. She left me and hidden. And she went by herself to see whether she could bring me back. And when she came back-- and see, nothing was really said directly to me. It's just that I hear-- heard these things. There was no one to bring me back to. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In fact, you know, we had a Czech neighbor. I don't know her name or anything, but this also Lena told me. And she walked over to her and she said, you know-- because my mother said that if anything happened, she's going to leave some things with this Czech woman. So she came in and she asked her, she said, you know, I need some things from the household. And the Czech woman said to her, leave right now. Because if you don't, I'm going to say that you have a Jew with you. And the only thing she gave her is like a spoon that I used to use. You know, a soup spoon, which I have to this day. And I don't know exactly where it is right now. But that's what Lena came home with. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well as I wanted to tell you, because they were afraid to keep me there, she had another sister by the name of Dunya that had a farm. In the Ukraine, they called it a [NON-ENGLISH], which means that it was a farm by itself not surrounded with other farms, very far from anything else. It was sort of all by itself with a lot of land. And they had horses, cows. I remember churning milk, and butter, and things like that. And so we went to stay with her, because we felt that that would be safe since there are no neighbors-- or at least immediate neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had Lena worked for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, it was forever. I mean, I don't remember ever being without her. So I don't know what year they were married or when they had my sister. But I was probably 13 or 12 at the time. So I would say at least 12 or 15 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was Lena at this point when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point-- you know, looking at the picture that I have of her-- I don't think she was more than like 30 or 35.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was never married?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was never married. Her whole life was devoted to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked you, if you can remember, the farm when you came in. What did it look like? Was it a big house? A little house? Was it a hut? How primitive--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was quite-- it was a house. It was-- you know, I guess like maybe a room and a half. Something like that. Maybe two rooms. You know, the main room was always with a big table and with this oven that I described to you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The farm itself in that place I do not remember, because as I said, I was not there that long. And I was hidden. I was not exposed out. So how could I know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there other children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No other children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the uncle, and her brother, and her mother treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. Very well. In fact, her mother-- you know, it's funny because she had like very big skirts. Several of them, you know. And I have a picture in my mind. She always brought me something. So she would pick up like several layers of skirts, and whatever it was-- whether it was candy, or nuts, or whatever-- would be in her-- in one of her skirt's pockets. And she would hand me candy.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Big farmers or just like--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think big farmers. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big was the village itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never was in that village, because I was hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I know. But when you like came to it, were there a lot of houses in there, or a little, a few?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess they was scattered, you know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that they did go to church. I don't know what they were. Whether they were Catholic or Protestant, I don't know. But they did go to church.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the period of time that you were there, did they take you with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not there. When I went to church was towards the end of the war, practically.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the few days or whatever length of time that you were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was-- it couldn't have been more than two, three weeks, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Lena explain your presence?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the mother?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: She told them the truth. She knew that they knew that I was not really her daughter. It's only for the outside world sort of that I was her daughter, illegitimate daughter, while she was away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had no papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had absolutely no papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she had never registered you anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you call her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I called her Lena.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have to call her mother or whatever the word would be?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't-- I couldn't that word, so I would just either touch her when I needed her or Lena. Not-- because I remembered my mother. So somehow, I guess, I couldn't say the word.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she tell you when she came back from Dubno when she tried to get you back to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think that she told me anything. I don't think that she-- I think that I found out later that, you know, what happened. She didn't say to me that, you know, they were destroyed or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- in this village and even subsequently-- but did you have any contact with anyone you knew there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody from Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: No one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- you had your sisters remained with-- and your brother too? Or the other young man that was part of the family? They were also in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were all in the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll get back to them a little later. How long did you stay in the village with Lena?

speaker: interviewee
text: In which are we talking?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the first one. The first village.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I mentioned three or something weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went to this [NON-ENGLISH] that I tried to explain to you that was a farm also. Bigger than this farm but isolated. With-- for miles you didn't have other farms. That's the reason that it was called a [NON-ENGLISH]. And there we stayed quite a number of months. I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it? Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good question. I don't remember the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it far?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was far from them. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if her brother drove us with a horse and carriage, you know. That's-- I believe that's how we got there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that point you were firmly her daughter-- her illegitimate daughter? They also knew. You see, the family-- the close family-- her family knew. Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was it. They just knew. And I was, you know, with them. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's farm was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This sister's.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dunya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dunya.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember her last name. She had only one son by the name of Urich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else lived on the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the father-- I think she had a husband. But you know, it's funny. I don't remember him. But I know she had a husband. A farmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else? Were there help on the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember any help. They just did their own thing. They had horses. They had cows. In fact, I used to go out with him to graze-- the cows to graze. And I remember churning butter. You know, that they made their own stuff. And I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Lena stay there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was Urich, the son?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, you know, I picture him like a boy of 14 or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was just a little older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, he was, you know, quite a bit older really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember about your stay at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that they were all very nice to me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Foods-- I don't remember. The only food I remember is that they used to have like a big-- how do you say, [YIDDISH]. Like a bowl--

speaker: interviewer
text: A bowl.

speaker: interviewee
text: With sour cream-- you know, their sour cream-- buttermilk, not sour cream. Full of, you know, buttermilk. Because their buttermilk was like in chunks. I remember that. Because other foods I don't remember. And they would boil potatoes. And that's what we used to eat. Like potatoes with this-- all from one bowl. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody had separate dishes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why I remember that, you know. But I don't remember anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you said your parents did not keep a kosher home. But were the foods very different and strange to you, or were they familiar foods?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- I never really paid attention to foods too much it seems. I don't remember ever being hungry. So you know, being on a farm you're really not hungry, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you sleep there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slept with Lena all the time. On some kind-- on a bed. I don't know which room or what. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language did you communicate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainian. Then by that-- you know, slowly I sort of picked it up. And each time more and more. And I did speak Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you become Mariusha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, immediately when we went to the first house-- to the first-- to her mother and brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she explain that to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- nobody explained anything. It's just-- I was Mariusha and that's it. It was not like somebody sat down and explained things to me. I think we just knew. I don't know. You just know these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any difficulty in responding to the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she ever call you Schifra?

speaker: interviewee
text: At night. She used to want to talk Jewish to me, and I didn't want to. That was later on. In the beginning, I don't think I talked too much. I don't know. You just existed, you know, more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any fears at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remembered the fears, first of all. You know, through them not wanting to keep us. And the fear-- I think I was very scared when we were walking with that police. You know, when they took us into that little police station. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That's-- you know, the fear, I think, was always there. You know, since I left you sort of have fear. What helped me was that I wasn't with a stranger, you know. And because I really loved her, that I think helped me not to be as afraid as I would if it was a total stranger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you ever afraid that you would respond to Schifra rather to-- rather than to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It never entered my mind. I don't think I cared. Because I remembered saying-- like she would say, you have to, you know, be quiet, or something like that, or hide. And I would say, so if it happens, it happens. I think I had that attitude.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remembered one time saying that. I think it was later on though when-- you know, during the war itself when the Russians were coming in. It's like I didn't care, you know? How did the family respond to the German occupation? The Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think that there were Germans around that farm. See, later on-- I don't know how long I was there, but I was there sort of the longest-- months, several months. I don't know if it was four, or six, or someplace around there. After that-- and I don't know the reason for it-- she had another sister that lived in a city called [NON-ENGLISH]. She was married, that sister, and lived in the city. There we-- for some reason, we went to her. I don't know the reason for it. See, like the first reason, I knew. The second reason, I didn't know. But all I know is that we went to live with Anya. Her name was Anya. And there I have to be hidden because her husband was a banderovets, which means like the equivalent of the SS but Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of house did she live in, Anya?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was sort of like an apartment, I think. It was very small. I remember it being very small. I remember there was a bed in the kitchen. And at night, I used to sleep with her. During the day, I was hidden also like in a little cellar. I don't know if you call it a cellar or a-- it wasn't a bunker because it was just there, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was underneath the apartment or in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Like underneath. Like you would have, let's say, under a stair or-- but it was more into the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just mostly during the day. And whenever-- if he drank, because he did drink-- they would say, get out of sight, you know. Hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew. He knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: He never gave me-- he knew. I think he-- I mean, it's not like I know from my own knowledge that he knew. But I'm assuming that he knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to change your tape.
